---
title: "Fleet Deployment"
---

Fleet deployment bridges the gap between a validated model checkpoint in the cloud and a running inference process on a physical robot. The Physical AI Toolchain implements this bridge through a GitOps architecture built on FluxCD, Azure Arc-connected edge clusters, and an automated deployment gating service that prevents untested models from reaching production hardware.

![Automated guided vehicles operating in a logistics warehouse represent the target deployment environment for fleet-scale robot policy rollout.
*Image: [Automated Guided Vehicle](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Automated_guided_vehicle.jpg) by Grendelkhan, [CC BY-SA 3.0](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/)*
](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/Automated_guided_vehicle.jpg/640px-Automated_guided_vehicle.jpg){fig-alt="Automated guided vehicle in a warehouse" width="70%"}

## GitOps Architecture

The deployment pipeline follows GitOps principles: a Git repository serves as the single source of truth for the desired state of every edge cluster. FluxCD controllers running on each Arc-connected device continuously reconcile the cluster state against the manifests in that repository. When a new model image appears in Azure Container Registry, an image automation controller detects the tag, commits an updated manifest to Git, and FluxCD propagates the change to every target cluster.

This architecture eliminates imperative `kubectl apply` commands from the deployment workflow entirely. Operators interact only with Git — pull requests serve as the change control mechanism, and the audit trail is the commit history.

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-gitops-pipeline
%%| fig-cap: "End-to-end GitOps pipeline from model registry through container build and FluxCD reconciliation to the edge fleet."
flowchart LR
    REG["Model Registry\n(ACR)"] --> SCAN["Image Reflector\nController"]
    SCAN --> POL["ImagePolicy\n(semver filter)"]
    POL --> AUTO["ImageUpdate\nAutomation"]
    AUTO --> GIT["Git Commit\n(manifest update)"]
    GIT --> SRC["FluxCD Source\nController"]
    SRC --> GATE{"Deployment\nGating Service"}
    GATE -->|approved| HELM["Helm / Kustomize\nController"]
    GATE -->|rejected| ALERT["Operator\nNotification"]
    HELM --> EDGE1["Edge Cluster A\n(K3s + Jetson)"]
    HELM --> EDGE2["Edge Cluster B\n(K3s + Jetson)"]
    HELM --> EDGE3["Edge Cluster N\n(K3s + Jetson)"]
    EDGE1 --> TEL["Fleet Intelligence\nTelemetry"]
    EDGE2 --> TEL
    EDGE3 --> TEL
```

## FluxCD Components

Six FluxCD custom resources form the deployment backbone:

| Resource | Role |
|----------|------|
| `GitRepository` | Points the source controller at the fleet manifests repository |
| `OCIRepository` | Tracks container image artifacts in Azure Container Registry |
| `HelmRelease` | Declares Helm chart deployments with values overrides per cluster |
| `Kustomization` | Applies Kustomize overlays for cluster-specific patches |
| `ImagePolicy` | Defines semver or regex filters for acceptable image tags |
| `ImageUpdateAutomation` | Commits updated image tags back to the Git repository |

A typical edge inference workload is deployed via a `HelmRelease` that references a chart stored alongside the fleet manifests:

```{bash}
#| eval: false
#| code-fold: false
# Example FluxCD HelmRelease for an ACT inference node on Jetson Orin
cat <<'EOF'
apiVersion: helm.toolkit.fluxcd.io/v2
kind: HelmRelease
metadata:
  name: act-inference
  namespace: robotics
spec:
  interval: 5m
  chart:
    spec:
      chart: ./charts/act-inference
      sourceRef:
        kind: GitRepository
        name: fleet-manifests
  values:
    image:
      repository: myacr.azurecr.io/act-inference
      tag: 1.4.2  # {"$imagepolicy": "flux-system:act-inference"}
    inference:
      policyRepo: "alizaidi/hve-robo-act-train"
      device: cuda
      controlHz: 30
      actionMode: absolute
    resources:
      limits:
        nvidia.com/gpu: "1"
        memory: 4Gi
      requests:
        memory: 2Gi
  upgrade:
    remediation:
      retries: 3
EOF
```

The `# {"$imagepolicy": ...}` marker tells the image update automation controller exactly which line to patch when a new tag matches the policy.

## Model Conversion and Container Packaging

Before deployment, trained PyTorch checkpoints undergo a conversion pipeline that targets the edge inference runtime:

1. **ONNX export** — The policy network is traced and exported to ONNX format with fixed batch size and input dimensions matching the robot's sensor configuration.
2. **TensorRT optimization** — The ONNX graph is compiled with `trtexec` targeting the Jetson Orin's GPU architecture (`sm_87`), producing an engine file with FP16 or INT8 precision.
3. **Container packaging** — The TensorRT engine, ROS 2 inference node, and runtime dependencies are packaged into a multi-architecture container image (arm64 for Jetson, amd64 for simulation).
4. **Registry push** — The image is tagged with a semver version and pushed to Azure Container Registry, triggering the image automation pipeline.

::: {.callout-tip}
## TensorRT Engine Portability
TensorRT engines are not portable across GPU architectures. Build separate engines for each target platform (e.g., Orin NX `sm_87` vs. desktop RTX `sm_89`). The container build pipeline handles this via multi-stage Docker builds with architecture-specific compilation stages.
:::

## Deployment Gating Service

The gating service intercepts FluxCD alert webhooks and blocks rollout until a model passes automated validation. When the image update automation commits a new tag, FluxCD generates a notification event. The gating service receives this event, retrieves evaluation metrics from MLflow, and makes an approve/reject decision.

| Gate Criterion | Source | Threshold | Operator |
|----------------|--------|-----------|----------|
| SiL success rate | MLflow experiment metrics | 95% | ≥ |
| Inference latency (p99) | Model evaluation pipeline | 50 ms | ≤ |
| Collision rate | SiL evaluation episodes | 1% | ≤ |
| Model artifact size | Container registry metadata | 500 MB | ≤ |

```{python}
#| eval: false
#| code-fold: false
import httpx

response = httpx.post(
    "https://gating-service.internal/api/v1/evaluate",
    json={
        "model_image": "myacr.azurecr.io/act-inference:1.4.2",
        "mlflow_run_id": "a1b2c3d4e5f6",
        "gates": {
            "sil_success_rate": {"threshold": 0.95, "operator": ">="},
            "latency_p99_ms": {"threshold": 50, "operator": "<="},
            "collision_rate": {"threshold": 0.01, "operator": "<="},
            "model_size_mb": {"threshold": 500, "operator": "<="},
        },
    },
)

result = response.json()
# {"approved": true, "scores": {"sil_success_rate": 0.97, ...}}
```

::: {.callout-warning}
## Manual Override
The gating service supports a manual approval endpoint for emergency deployments. Manual overrides are logged with operator identity and bypass reason for audit compliance. Use sparingly — bypassed models skip safety validation entirely.
:::

## Rollout Strategies

The toolchain supports two rollout strategies for fleet-wide updates:

**Canary deployment** — A subset of edge clusters (typically 5–10%) receives the update first. The fleet intelligence pipeline monitors telemetry from canary devices for a configurable soak period (default: 4 hours). If drift detection or error rate thresholds trigger, the rollout halts automatically and the canary clusters roll back to the previous revision.

**Rolling update** — After canary validation passes, the remaining fleet receives the update in configurable batch sizes. FluxCD's `maxUnavailable` setting controls how many devices update simultaneously, ensuring minimum fleet capacity is maintained throughout the rollout window.

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-rollout-strategy
%%| fig-cap: "Canary-then-rolling rollout strategy with automated soak period validation."
flowchart TD
    NEW["New Model\nApproved"] --> CANARY["Deploy to\nCanary Fleet (5%)"]
    CANARY --> SOAK["Soak Period\n(4 hours)"]
    SOAK --> CHECK{"Telemetry\nHealthy?"}
    CHECK -->|yes| ROLL["Rolling Update\nRemaining 95%"]
    CHECK -->|no| BACK["Rollback\nCanary Fleet"]
    BACK --> NOTIFY["Alert Operator\n+ Log Failure"]
    ROLL --> DONE["Fleet Updated"]
```

## Cluster Overlay Structure

Per-cluster customization uses Kustomize overlays stored in the Git repository under `gitops/clusters/`. Each cluster directory contains patches for resource limits, node selectors, and environment-specific configuration. This structure allows a single base chart to serve heterogeneous edge hardware — from Jetson Orin Nano devices running lightweight policies to AGX Orin units running full sensor fusion stacks.

::: {.callout-note}
## Azure Arc Integration
Each edge cluster runs Azure Arc agents that provide centralized inventory, policy compliance, and GitOps configuration from the Azure portal. Arc-connected clusters appear in Azure Resource Manager alongside cloud resources, enabling unified RBAC and monitoring across the entire fleet.
:::

## Inference Runtime

The deployed inference container runs an **ACT Inference Node** as a ROS 2 lifecycle node. It subscribes to `/joint_states` at 100 Hz and `/camera/color/image_raw` at 30 Hz, executes the policy forward pass on the Jetson GPU, and publishes joint trajectory commands at 30 Hz on `/lerobot/joint_commands`. A safety-gated `enable_control` parameter allows operators to disable command publishing without stopping the node, useful during commissioning and debugging on live hardware.
